notes: 15-clue draft for music_performing_arts (17 active clues at time of authoring — tied for thinnest non-geography category). All clues sourced from data/sourcing/docs/music_performing_arts/. Subcategory spread: Popular Music (6), Film Scores & Soundtracks (4), Music Theory & Terms (2), Broadway & Musicals (1), Classical Composers (1), Instruments (1). Previously missing subcategories now covered: Film Scores & Soundtracks, Instruments, Music Theory & Terms. Hard tail: Diana Ross $800 (first African American actress nominated for Academy Award for debut film performance), Shake Your Booty $800 (B-side connection), Stop Making Sense $1000 (Demme / independently financed / Pantages / digital audio). Avoided all active + pending answers: Beethoven's Fifth, 1812 Overture, Boléro, Swan Lake, Maurice Ravel, Gustav Mahler, Arnold Schoenberg, John Coltrane, Bayreuth, The Beatles, Shaggy, Tommy Tutone, Skiffle, Sweeney Todd, Annie Oakley, Oh Mary!, Hedwig, Annie Get Your Gun, Big Lebowski.

questions.clue: Set in 1913 to the traditional Irish melody of the Londonderry Air, this beloved folk ballad's lyrics were written three years earlier by English lawyer Frederic Weatherly.

citations.title: Danny Boy
citations.snippet: "Danny Boy" is a folk song with lyrics written by English lawyer Frederic Weatherly in 1910, and set to the traditional Irish melody of "Londonderry Air" in 1913.

questions.clue: This professional, commonly abbreviated to two letters, originally took their title from the shellac records they spun; today the term encompasses radio hosts, club performers, and anyone who mixes recorded music for audiences.

citations.title: Disc jockey
citations.snippet: A disc jockey, more commonly abbreviated as DJ, is a person who plays recorded music for an audience. Types of DJs include radio DJs, club DJs, mobile DJs, and turntablists. Originally, the "disc" in "disc jockey" referred to shellac and la

questions.clue: The final film in a trilogy by director Baz Luhrmann, this 2001 jukebox musical set in Paris's Montmartre Quarter starred Nicole Kidman and Ewan McGregor and received eight Academy Award nominations.

citations.title: Moulin Rouge!
citations.snippet: Moulin Rouge! is a 2001 jukebox musical romantic drama film directed, produced, and co-written by Baz Luhrmann. It follows an English poet, Christian, who falls in love with the star of the Moulin Rouge, cabaret actress and courtesan Satine

questions.clue: Born Chaim Witz in Israel in 1949, this musician co-founded a hard rock band celebrated for its elaborate stage makeup and personas in 1973, serving as its bassist and co-lead singer until the group's dissolution five decades later.

citations.title: Gene Simmons
citations.snippet: Gene Simmons, also known by his stage persona "the Demon", is an Israeli and American musician. He was the bassist and co-lead singer of the hard rock band Kiss, which he co-founded with Paul Stanley, Ace Frehley, and Peter Criss in 1973. S

questions.clue: Composed by Nile Rodgers and Bernard Edwards and initially rejected by Atlantic Records, this 1979 disco anthem became the signature song of the sibling vocal group Sister Sledge and was preserved in the National Recording Registry in 2017.

citations.title: We Are Family (song)
citations.snippet: "We Are Family" is a song recorded by American sibling vocal group Sister Sledge. Composed by Bernard Edwards and Nile Rodgers, they both offered the song to Atlantic Records; although the record label initially declined, the track was rele

questions.clue: In music, this term names a formation of exactly seven performers, and can also apply to a seven-line stanza of poetry.

citations.title: Septet
citations.snippet: A septet is a formation containing exactly seven members. It is commonly associated with musical groups but can be applied to any situation where seven similar or related objects are considered a single unit, such as a seven-line stanza of

questions.clue: Composed of roughly two-thirds copper and one-third zinc, this alloy has been used since prehistoric times and lends its name to the orchestral family that includes the trumpet, French horn, trombone, and tuba.

citations.title: Brass
citations.snippet: Brass is an alloy of copper and zinc, in proportions which can be varied to achieve different colours and mechanical, electrical, acoustic, and chemical properties, but copper typically has the larger proportion, generally 2⁄3 copper and 1⁄

questions.clue: This Stephen Sondheim musical, which intertwines Brothers Grimm fairy tales around a childless baker and his wife, debuted on Broadway in November 1987 and won Tony Awards for Best Score and Best Book in a season dominated by Andrew Lloyd Webber's longest-running production.

citations.title: Into the Woods
citations.snippet: Into the Woods is a 1986 musical with music and lyrics by Stephen Sondheim and book by James Lapine.

questions.clue: Written by Friedrich Schiller in the summer of 1785 and published in the German magazine Thalia the following year, this poem's melody was adopted without its original text as the Anthem of Europe by the Council of Europe in 1972.

citations.title: Ode to Joy
citations.snippet: "Ode to Joy" is an ode written in the summer of 1785 by the German poet, playwright, and historian Friedrich Schiller. It was published the following year in the German magazine Thalia. In 1808, a slightly revised version changed two lines

questions.clue: Conceived by French producers Jacques Morali and Henri Belolo and named for a Manhattan neighborhood known as a gayborhood, this disco group's biggest hit was described by the Library of Congress in 2020 as 'an American phenomenon' and added to the National Recording Registry.

citations.title: Village People
citations.snippet: Village People is an American disco group known for its on-stage costumes and suggestive lyrics in their music. The group was originally formed by French producers Jacques Morali and Henri Belolo and lead singer Victor Willis following the

questions.clue: The first African American actress nominated for an Academy Award for a debut film performance, this Motown star's 1972 portrayal of a jazz legend yielded the only solo album of her career to reach number one on the U.S. Billboard 200.

citations.title: Diana Ross
citations.snippet: Diana Ross is an American singer and actress. She was the lead singer of the vocal group the Supremes, who became Motown's most successful act during the 1960s and one of the world's best-selling girl groups of all time. They remain the bes

questions.clue: The B-side of this 1976 KC and the Sunshine Band chart-topper was "Boogie Shoes," which only became a hit after appearing on a disco film soundtrack the following year.

citations.title: (Shake, Shake, Shake) Shake Your Booty
citations.snippet: "(Shake, Shake, Shake) Shake Your Booty" is a song recorded and released in 1976 by KC and the Sunshine Band for the album Part 3. The song became their third number-one hit on the Billboard Hot 100, as well as their third number-one on the

questions.clue: Directed by Jonathan Demme and independently financed for $1.2 million by the four-member band it documents, this 1984 concert film was shot over four nights at Hollywood's Pantages Theatre and is considered a pioneering example of early digital audio techniques in film.

citations.title: Stop Making Sense
citations.snippet: Stop Making Sense is a 1984 American concert film featuring a live performance by the American rock band Talking Heads. The film was directed by Jonathan Demme and executive produced by Gary Kurfirst, the band’s longtime manager. The film w
